title: Eugenia neoriedeliana
intro: Eugenia neoriedeliana, commonly known as the Brazilian guava, is a species of flowering plant in the myrtle family (Myrtaceae) that is native to Brazil. The plant has been reported to possess various health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which may help to protect against diseases such as diabetes, hypertension, and certain types of cancer. The bioactive constituents of E. neoriedeliana include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, the leaves and fruits of E. neoriedeliana are often used to make teas, infusions, and decoctions, as well as being incorporated into topical creams and ointments to treat skin conditions such as wounds and eczema.
taxonomy: Eugenia neoriedeliana is classified within the family Myrtaceae. This family is commonly known as the myrtle family and consists of around 150 genera and 6,000 species. Eugenia neoriedeliana is further classified within the genus Eugenia, which includes approximately 600 species. The genus Eugenia is characterized by its evergreen trees and shrubs, often found in tropical and subtropical regions. These plants are commonly known as surawong, guinep, or pomme-cerise, depending on the region.
uses: Eugenia neoriedeliana, a plant species, has been found to possess various medicinal properties. The extracts of Eugenia neoriedeliana have been studied for their potential to exhibit anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial activities. Research suggests that the plant may have applications in treating various health conditions, including infections, inflammation, and oxidative stress.
